title: North Korea as It Is: The Chilling Case for a Cold Peace
content:
In the early 1990s, the United States began to realize that North Korea would be the world's next nuclear threat. At the time, North Korea barely had enough fissile material to build one or two crude bombs. It lacked the delivery systems that would allow such weapons to reach the United States. Yet observant officials recognized that North Korea was intent on obtaining nuclear weapons and becoming a source of regional instability.

Three and a half decades later, North Korea has blown past even the most pessimistic predictions. It has amassed 50 nuclear bombs and stockpiled enough material to build many more. It has developed nearly 20 different delivery systems, including long-range intercontinental ballistic missiles. The country is actively pursuing ballistic missiles that can be launched from nuclear submarines. Pyongyang has tested its nuclear weapons six times and its various delivery systems more than 300 times.

North Korean leader Kim Jong Un intends to develop a modern nuclear weapons arsenal the size of France or the United Kingdom. In return for supporting Russia's war in Ukraine, Moscow is helping Pyongyang surmount technological hurdles. Since the threat emerged, U.S. strategy has been driven by the logic of complete, verifiable, and irreversible denuclearization. Washington has offered incremental incentives in exchange for nuclear concessions.

The United States has also relied heavily on economic sanctions to bring North Korea to the negotiating table. However, the size and sophistication of North Korea's current nuclear arsenal show that these approaches have failed. The U.S. has struggled to implement agreements due to a lack of consistent attention and partisan divides. Sanctions have only hardened Pyongyang's resolve to expand its military capabilities.
